g.data-narration: Welcome to Exception Handling in ARM Cortex-M. This module covers interrupts, faults, and the NVIC architecture.
An exception is an event that disrupts normal program flow, requiring the CPU to execute special handling code immediately.
Exception handling enables real-time response to external events while maintaining deterministic program execution.
g.text: Embedded systems must respond to events within strict time constraints while executing main program logic.
Exception handling bridges asynchronous events with deterministic code execution.

g.data-narration: ARM Cortex-M defines multiple exception types from Reset (highest priority) to external interrupts (IRQs).
Lower priority numbers mean higher priority. Negative values are fixed priority (non-configurable).
g.text: Lower numerical value = Higher priority (more urgent)

g.data-narration: The vector table maps exception numbers to handler addresses at memory location 0x00000000.
The Nested Vectored Interrupt Controller manages interrupt enabling, priority, and pending status for all IRQ sources.
g.text: Integrated in Cortex-M, manages all IRQ prioritization and enabling

g.data-narration: Exception handling follows four stages: Entry saves state, Vector Fetch loads handler, Handler executes, then Exit restores state.
During exception entry, the processor automatically pushes eight registers plus xPSR onto the stack.
g.text: Processor automatically saves this state when exception occurs

g.data-narration: GPIO external interrupts require configuring the pin, EXTI line, and NVIC for complete setup.
ISRs require special function attributes and should be short, non-blocking, and clear pending bits before exit.
Tail chaining reduces latency by transitioning directly between exceptions. Late arriving handles higher priority exceptions during stacking.

g.text: Direct transition between exceptions → reduces latency
Higher priority arrives during stacking → restart

g.data-narration: Priority grouping splits priority bits into group priority (for preemption) and sub-priority (for tie-breaking).
Fault exceptions handle system errors: Hard Fault (fixed priority), Memory/Bus/Usage Faults (configurable).
